one_line_takeaway: By exploiting sequential model releases and controlling the canary's insertion time, the optimal likelihood-ratio SeMI attack keeps its detection power constant as more snapshots are released (unlike a final-snapshot-only attack, whose power dilutes with T), yielding measurably tighter DP-SGD privacy audits (e.g., AUC 0.883 vs. 0.610-0.770 for baselines in the non-private case) than heuristic multi-snapshot score-combination methods.
key_finding: On DP-SGD/Fashion-MNIST at T=5 non-private (epsilon=infinity), SeMISGD achieves AUC=0.883 versus Loss Diff AUC=0.746, Loss Ratio AUC=0.749, SeMISGD_max AUC=0.824, Delta Ratio AUC=0.732, Delta Diff AUC=0.727, Back-Front Ratio AUC=0.610, Back-Front Diff AUC=0.718, and LiRA AUC=0.770 (Figure 4a). Under DP-SGD at epsilon=8, SeMISGD AUC drops to 0.623 but still leads Loss Diff (0.534), Loss Ratio (0.533), SeMISGD_max (0.608), Delta Ratio (0.532), Delta Diff (0.530), Back-Front Ratio (0.506), Back-Front Diff (0.510), and LiRA (0.504) (Figure 4b). In the privacy-audit experiments (epsilon=8, delta=1e-4), SeMISGD dominates every baseline's empirical lower bound epsilon-hat across T in {2,5,10} (Observation 1); among informed attacks SeMISGD has the tightest median epsilon-hat over Loss Diff/Loss Ratio (Observation 2); each known-tau attack yields a tighter bound than its tau-agnostic counterpart, e.g. SeMISGD vs. SeMISGD_max, Loss Diff vs. Delta Diff, Loss Ratio vs. Delta Ratio (Observation 3); and sweeping tau across T=5 phases shows audit tightness is strongly tau-dependent, with later insertion times giving tighter bounds for SeMISGD because the per-transition signal grows once the model has moved away from initialization (Observation 4).
core_contribution: The paper introduces Sequential Membership Inference (SeMI) attacks, which exploit the fact that a target model is not released once but as a sequence of T snapshots over training/fine-tuning phases, plus a controllable canary insertion time tau. The authors (1) derive SeMI*, the Neyman-Pearson-optimal likelihood-ratio (LR) test for detecting a canary inserted at a known step tau, in closed form for the empirical-mean mechanism on Gaussian data (Theorem 3.1), and prove an 'isolation property' (Lemma 3.2): the LR over the whole sequence collapses to a single transition p(mu_hat_tau | mu_hat_tau-1), so the attack's power depends only on batch size n and the target's Mahalanobis distance m*, not on tau or T -- in contrast to a 'Final Observation' baseline that pools the target among nT samples and whose power dilutes as T grows (Lemma 3.4). (2) They extend this to (DP-)SGD via a batch-gradient Gaussianity assumption, deriving SeMISGD (Theorem 4.1), a white-box Mahalanobis-weighted score on the parameter update at the insertion transition, plus two black-box loss-based surrogates (Loss Diff, Loss Ratio) that generalize the Back-Front/Delta heuristics of Jagielski et al. (JWO+23) as restrictions to the known insertion phase. (3) They formalize a sequential DP-audit procedure (Lemma 2.1, Algorithm 2) that turns SeMI attack Type-I/II errors into an epsilon lower bound, using a calibrated-threshold-grid Clopper-Pearson scheme, and empirically evaluate it on softmax regression trained with DP-SGD on Fashion-MNIST.
threat_model: White-box and black-box auditor/adversary who observes a sequence of T released model snapshots (theta_0 ... theta_T) from a mechanism trained/fine-tuned in successive phases (e.g., versioned foundation models, fine-tunes, federated rounds). The auditor/crafter controls (a) the injected canary z* and (b) the insertion time tau (which training phase/batch the canary enters), and must decide, from the observed outputs, whether z* was inserted at tau (H1) or not (H0). White-box variant observes model parameters/gradients at each step; black-box variant observes only the target's loss at each released checkpoint. This is an auditing/privacy-leakage threat model (the 'adversary' is typically the auditor acting adversarially against their own system), not an attacker seeking to exfiltrate secrets from a third party.
limitations: The closed-form optimality analysis (SeMI*, isolation property, error formulas) is derived only for the empirical-mean-of-Gaussian-data mechanism; the SGD extension relies on an unverified batch-gradient Gaussianity assumption justified only heuristically via CLT intuition. The synthetic numerical illustration in Section 3.3 explicitly departs from the i.i.d. disjoint-batch assumption underlying SeMI* (batches are subsampled with replacement from a size-20 base set, so batches share/duplicate entries), a mismatch the authors acknowledge and only partially address by also reporting a matching disjoint-batch protocol in an appendix. Choosing a good insertion time tau a priori for a given attack/training protocol is stated as an open problem. Main-text DP-SGD experiments are limited to a single model/dataset (softmax regression on Fashion-MNIST); broader coverage (CIFAR-10, Purchase-100, a pretrain-then-finetune setup) is deferred to an appendix not captured in this extraction. The multi-insertion-time and distribution-shift extensions are stated as direct corollaries of the isolation property but their empirical validation is deferred to appendices.

datasets_used: Fashion-MNIST (main-text DP-SGD audits: softmax regression, L=2500 steps)
synthetic Gaussian data N(0,1) for empirical-mean mechanism illustration (base size N=20)
CIFAR-10 (appendix fine-tuning variant, not in extracted text)
Purchase-100 (appendix fine-tuning variant, not in extracted text)
quantitative_results: Non-private (epsilon=infinity) AUC at T=5: SeMISGD 0.883, SeMISGD_max 0.824, LiRA 0.770, Loss Ratio 0.749, Loss Diff 0.746, Delta Ratio 0.732, Back-Front Diff 0.718, Delta Diff 0.727, Back-Front Ratio 0.610
Private (epsilon=8) AUC at T=5: SeMISGD 0.623, SeMISGD_max 0.608, Loss Diff 0.534, Loss Ratio 0.533, Delta Ratio 0.532, Delta Diff 0.530, Back-Front Ratio 0.506, Back-Front Diff 0.510, LiRA 0.504

DP-SGD audit config: epsilon=8 (total budget), delta=1e-4, clipping norm c=1, learning rate=0.01, T in {2,5,10}, R=1900 audit rounds, Rcal=100 calibration rounds, K=99 threshold grid, overall confidence 1-xi=0.98
Synthetic empirical-mean illustration: N=20 base samples, T=10 batches of n=10 (sampled with replacement), target Mahalanobis distance m*=5, insertion tau=5, R=50,000 runs
baselines_compared: Final Observation (single-snapshot MI attack, this paper's own theoretical baseline)
Delta Diff / Delta Ratio (uninformed sequential heuristic, from Jagielski et al. 2023)
Back-Front Diff / Back-Front Ratio (uninformed sequential heuristic, from Jagielski et al. 2023)
SeMISGD_max (this paper's own tau-agnostic GLR variant)
